text: How are you?
I can't complain.
Doing okay.
Sorry, I'm just pulling up your resume again.
Yeah, no problem.
But anyway, so I will give you, to start, I'll give you a little bit of on growthx, and then we can dive into your experience.
But I started at growthx maybe two and a half months ago, and we were probably only 20 people then, now we have 50 people in the company, which is pretty crazy.
And a lot of those people are on, like, the client support sort of, like, customer-facing team.
So we, the company itself is, today it looks mostly like an agency setup where we're doing a lot of content and SEO engagements for big brands.
And then on the back end, we're using our AI work.
Flows that we've built to produce all of the content.
So we have like a big layer of people who are kind of ensuring that what the AI writes and creates and all the prompting and stuff is producing a quality end product.
So we, yeah, the company itself is only maybe eight months old and we have now 40 customers and 7 million in AR.
So growing super fast and then the team is growing.
So with that comes, you know, a lot of good chaos, but like, yeah, just hard to get everybody doing the same things and having visibility into what everybody is doing.
And then like the eventual goal, obviously, is to create like a ton of repeatability.
So right now it's, there's just a lot of things that can be improved.
And so I rewrote the job description last week for this.
I just wanted to make it clear.
And I've had some thoughts since I wrote it.
So I want to just let you know of the other angles I'm thinking.
Because I've been talking to our CTO a lot about it.
And it really has this heavy program management angle to it, which is that there's almost two different product interfaces, I'll call it.
We have our internal team working with our AI workflows.
So that's one product and user, right?
And that's all internal.
And are they using it right?
Are they using it at all?
Are they providing feedback to the engineering team?
Are they doing what they're supposed to be doing?
Like, there's a lot of programs that can be built around that.
And then there's a second part of it, though, which is like, obviously, whatever we produce, like that package.
The external world, how are we showing our customers, how often are we showing stuff, how do we demonstrate value on a regular basis, like what are the touch points we need to have.
So it really is a big scope, I'm looking for someone who can be like, one, understand strategy level.

Like that has to be like, obviously, like you can't approach things and build like process and programs without understanding like where we're going and what the customer actually wants at the end of the day.
Like there's no, the, our team using our workflows is like the means to the end, which is like happy customers.
And then, and then the other piece is just like, so, so, and then like actually someone who is used to like implementing new processes or whatever.
Yeah.
The team's like, the customer facing team is probably 30 people now.

And like, the goal, the plan in the near term is to have almost, there's three different layers, like levels of people that interact with customers, but the bottom level, I'll call it, they're like the content managers are the one like really running the workflows.
We almost want to get to a point where it's like one to one with customers.
So like, you know, if we have 70 customers, there's 70 of those content managers.
And large, these teams are largely outside of North America, as well.
So that helps us like business wise scaling.
And we're using a lot of like different avenues to find these people.
Yeah, they're awesome.
But yeah, that's, that's kind of like the gist.
The gist.
So I'm curious to hear your thoughts and questions, of course.
And then I'd also like, obviously, we can flip it and I'd love to hear about your chat.
Yeah.
No real questions about it.
I did notice the job title changed over the past week.
So I mean, even the previous one and the current one that kind of like tracks like everything you said, I envisioned for it.
But so, yeah, I think we're pretty aligned on like what the expectations and where the fit is within.
Okay.
Yeah, I was getting like a really mixed bag of applicants before.
And like, I have been flip flopping about who what kind of profile would be perfect.
And it's like, there's sort of like this RevOps mindset angle to it, where like generally RevOps people, like, it's super strategic, and you need to build systems.
Right.
But I find sometimes they over index on tools, where there's just a lot of humans and like human process and things.
Yeah, totally.
So then I like, tried to lean back towards like, this program management world, or somebody who at least has been like, has a deeper understanding of like, yeah, yeah, that makes sense.
Yeah, no, I think there's a pretty good overlap with kind of my career and my interests and what this role is looking for.
Walk me through it.
give me the high level of your path and what you're looking for next.
Yeah, so I'm originally a musician and turned entrepreneur and business person.
What instrument?
Percussion.
So a lot of instruments at the end of the day.
So just being able to hit things in the right rhythm.
But yeah, I kind of started out my life as a musician, and I went to like my undergrad degrees in music.
And I kind of determined at the end of that, that this isn't the career path I'm looking for.
I want to pursue music more as a hobby and have a career that like supports that hobby financially.
Basically, I mean, for.
Cushion is a very expensive instrument to be involved in.
Like some of these things cost like tens of thousands of dollars.
So like if you even want one, you've got to kind of make the money to get it.
So I looked at like options to shift into business, but also stay on like the creative side of things because I had a very creative mind coming out of music.
And that's where entrepreneurship kind of came into play.
So I did like almost a double major in my undergrad, and then I took that to a master's in entrepreneurship up at the University of Washington.
Nice.
Where are you based, by the way?
I didn't even ask.
I live in Seattle.
Okay, cool.
Yeah.
Pacific Northwestern.
I mean, I was born in Texas, but I'm definitely converted.
Nice.
I'm born here, so.
Yeah, this is the longest I've been in one place.
So I say this is home now at this point.
And it's where I've been like most of my adult life.
So it kind of makes sense.
And I love it up here.
Lots of good energy.
So yeah, the master's degree brought me up here.
I decided to stay.
And kind of keep...
Thank
pursuing a tech career.
During the master's degree and kind of the first couple of years afterwards, I was very, very heavily involved in the entrepreneurship scene here in Seattle.
I've been on advisory boards and board of directors for multiple startups that have come through like business competitions and pitch competitions and that kind of stuff.
I've participated in those as well with my own ideas.
I think I won the first Amazon Pitch Weekend contest.
They kind of like brought a bunch of people in.

were like form teams of five to eight and pick someone's idea and like basically create, you know, a value proposition, business plan, like everything you'd need to like get it off the ground and put it in front of investors.
Awesome.
What was the idea?
It was one of the team members had the idea.
I ended up being the team leader and kind of presenter.
So I did like the 10 minute pitch at the end of the weekend.
The idea was it was using like, this is kind of when 3D printing was taking off, like the the machines were getting smaller and

So she had an idea to make accessible, how to put it, 3D printed accessibility features for museums and like galleries where someone who maybe can't see could feel like what they're supposed to be looking at, like either through Braille describing it, but also like literally a small model of like a sculpture that they could touch.
Like Michelangelo's David sculpture would have like a little 3D printed accessible thing that they could feel and they could see it in their mind's eye, right?
So it was such a cool idea.
We had a lot of energy around it.
But the girl who had the idea was from Hawaii.
So she ultimately went back to Hawaii and didn't pursue it.
But it was a really fun weekend.
We got like a co-working space credit as an award.
So I had like six months of co-working space access from it.
It was fun or was that I did it?
Yeah, totally for fun.
Completely.
I was there was so much like startup energy in Seattle.
Eight years ago when this was happening, this was like before Uber was public, like there were so many unicorns cropping up around the country.
So there was a lot of energy up here.
So I was very happy to be involved in that.
One of my favorite things after my grad program was working with the young professionals of Seattle org.

So I joined their board as like the operations person and basically helped facilitate, manage, crop up networking opportunities for young professionals in the area, which included a lot of entrepreneurial people and startup people, people coming from California, moving up here for what's going on.
So we had like a couple networking events per month would pull in like 250 plus people and then like some biannual like galas and stuff.
was super fun.
But then on top of all of that, I actually had my career starting up, which was just such a busy, like fun time of my life.
But yeah, at the same time, I joined one of my teachers from my master's degree.
was a teaching assistant in the entrepreneurship program at UW.

And he was like, hey, this is kind of entrepreneurial, but it's also at an established company.
I want you to come on and be like my right hand man.
So I joined him.
He was the head of CS at the time.
Was this Ignite?
No, this is Avanti Markets.
So it was an unattended smart vending machine, like retail market solution.
So imagine like a kiosk that you scan the product and you check out like a self-checkout.
But it was a smart vending machine with like new technology in it, like weight sensors and lasers.
And it was really cool.
I had to learn a lot about this kind of hardware to support it.
But we were launching this new product.
was a hardware and software mixture building out this.
CS department at the same time for the company because they've never had to do a SaaS kind of model.
So we needed new systems, new telemetry, like understanding what a healthy customer looks like, building health models, like everything under the sun that you could think of in CS.
We had to build from the ground up.
So it was a really like a good kickstart to my career.
was a very involved and like engaging and good learning opportunity to start out with.
After that one, I kind of thought I want to be a little more understanding of like what goes into the data structures that support a good customer success operations org.
So I shifted towards being an analyst and that's where my Ignite position was.
So there I kind of was more on like the reporting side of things, understanding KPIs, like internal metrics for performance, external metrics for performance.
What the customer wants, what their sentiment is, how that plays into the CSMs and their conversations.
What kind of product is Ignite?
Ignite's a file management system.
It's like SharePoint, but they have like government pre-approvals.
So they're like a secure file system where like a customer of Ignite, if they have a government contract, they don't have to work through like the approval processes to store sensitive data.
So that was kind of like their value angle in the landscape.
Obviously, there's tons of file management systems like Dropbox and yeah.
So it's SaaS, like it's like a traditional SaaS DSM.
Yeah, full SaaS.
So I got the hardware experience in my first job and then I switched to a full SaaS company during COVID.
And that was a great experience kind of building out the reports, enhancing my analytical skills, learning like SQL and Python and that kind of stuff.
Really getting a grasp of what goes into building the workflows that I was doing at the job before and then inevitably what I ended up doing at F5 as a project.
Program manager.
So that was the past year of my life, but I kind of determined that workplace size, scale, the vibe there was not exactly what I was looking for.
So I just kind of made the personal decision to step back, look for something new, something, you know, on the earlier side of things.
I really like, you know, scaling, high growth, high intensity environments.
So that's why I stepped back away from that and I'm looking for something like this.
What, when you went to F5, what were you looking to get out of it?
I was expecting a bit more of a strategic seat at the table, to be honest.
Yeah, it was very much like, there were a lot of layers to leadership above the program manager role.
So things tended to be very, very filtered on their way down from the top.
And I wasn't able to kind of contribute my broad experience building out these different CS environments.
It was very much just an admin.
Like, here's the task, do the task kind of.
position.
I'm great with the program management stuff, but it's just the daily, like, here's your task list.
don't really have the ability, the agency to...
Yeah, to do, yeah.
I mean, I could not, the biggest company I've been at was like 700 people, and I thought that was impressive.

It was 800 when I joined, and then 1,000 when I left.
They were scaling up pretty intensely.
Okay, cool.
think plateaued around there.
The first company was like 40 people.
That was a much smaller...

And what kind of, like, programs have you run?
Maybe just pick an example, and we can talk through one.
Yeah, I mean, at F5, I basically built out the entire customer journey in Gainsight and Salesforce and the other tools that we were using.
At the time, so it was like a six-month program working with the CSM managers and the data team.
And just kind of establishing what are the things we're expecting at what times, right?
There's like a deployment phase, there's a usage and expansion phase, and a renewal phase.
Where are we asking of the CSMs at what times?
What kind of conversations are they having with the customers?
What kind of metrics are we tracking and reporting?
So nothing existed before?
They had tons of automations and tasks and such, but they weren't tied to journey stages.
People generally understood that there's a kickoff, like onboarding phase, but it was never defined in Gainsight.
It wasn't pooled properly.

So I was kind of taking a lot of existing technical debt, parsing through it, understanding what is relevant, what's not being used, and then creating some net new stuff that would kind of work complementarily through what they already have established.
Okay.
And what was like from the CSMs?
Just personalised.
Perspective, like, or even for like, I don't know, from different people's perspectives, what was like the pain that was felt before all the fixes?
I think it was maybe just a mismatch on expectations through the customer's contract cycle between different CSMs.
It was a very large CSM organization.
It was global as well.
So there's a lot of different cultures playing into it.

But we didn't see a lot of consistency across the board in terms of like, what the CSMs are even saying on calls, like the verbiage they're using, the type of sentiment they're, you know, getting from the customer and how they log in in GameSite, how it compares to the other CSMs.
And the customer journey framework helped kind of build a foundation that they didn't have before.
How did you know, like, and maybe this was before your time, but how did they know that people were not doing things?
Partially.

Because it's tracked through a system like Gong, where their conversations are literally transcribed, every single one of them, and you can use AI to parse through that and kind of say, out of these transcripts, what are the similarities, what are the big differences, and then that'll give you some starting points for the conversations.
Okay.
And so that was running at scale?
That was running at scale?
Yeah, yeah.
We had implemented Gong a few years ago before I joined, and then the AI features kind of started getting a lot more prominent the past year or so.
We've seen kind of an explosion in what's available.
So even just using the GPT models that are available now, you can do really robust things with it.
Yeah, I've seen it.
I've seen like it won off, but I've never seen, I mean, I've also only been to small companies.
I've never seen like anybody like put it into production, like read every customer call.
And summarize the sentiment and produce a rating or something like that.
Yeah, at Ignite, we actually built it from scratch.
My team's data scientist was very, very proficient in Python.
And he was able to write a language model that scraped the Gong transcripts for, like, how likely is this customer to turn on a scale of, like, 1 to 10.
And we would push that into, like, GainSight, and that would be on the customer profile for the CSM.
It was, like, Gong sentiment score.
And then we'd have that internally, too, for, like, is this CSM saying this thing?
And that's leading to a higher likelihood of churn or not.
Yeah.
So back to your program that you implemented, so it was, like, tying different phases of the lifecycle to, like, tasks in GainSight almost?
Like, you need to...
Yeah, actions at expected times.
Expected or required?
Like, how does that work?
Well, you can...
They can require it, but there can always be, you know, like poor performers or bad actors that don't fall wrong or they don't think that it's enforced in the way.
You really have to have leadership buy-in and leadership oversight for these things to be required, I've found, that people will always skirt the admin responsibilities if they can.
So what did the company do when people were like, not really taking the recommendations, right?

Like we, I'm asking because we sort of have like similar things here or we could where it's like, here's task by task, like recommended what you should do in the first 12 weeks, seven, eight of the engagement with the agency.
But it's not like, you know, people, some people just don't.
It's like, why didn't you do, you know, so it's like, it always kind of blows my mind that people just choose to omit that from like, they just don't do it.
But, but it happens at F5, it was tied to their bonus structure.
So there was a financial incentive to add.

As we were updating the journey and making these things more defined across the board, our quarterly goals on the personal level were changing as well for the CSMs to match some of that stuff as well.
So it's sort of like an OKR-ish type payoff.
Yeah, yeah, exactly.
Yeah, it's kind of hard to prescribe OKRs in customer success.
It's not as clear cut as sales and renewals.
So that seemed to work pretty well.
And honestly, reducing the task was a big part of it.
Like we had a lot of tasks that were very similar to one another, like stepping on each other's toes.
There's multiple people had come through F5 over the past like six years of their gain site implementation.
And maybe the second person in doesn't realize that the first person also created this thing that they think is a good idea.
So then they make it again.
And then you have multiples of these things stacking up.
And it gets to the point where it's so noisy that the CSM is completely discouraged from even like attempting to do the.
How would you, so say some CSM came to you and was like, bitching about that.
Yeah.
What would you do?
I usually would, I've had it happen many, many times.
I would get on a screen share with them and have them show me their workflow and where they encountered that from a user experience perspective.
Like where did that show up in their view?
Maybe it's just something CS ops didn't have a lens on.
Like they're not, we're weren't looking in that part of the system.
So that's produced a lot of like, wow, we probably should do an audit of this kind of thing after they show me on their screen.
Yeah.
But typically a collaborative approach on problem solving like that is the fastest resolution in my experience.
And would you be comfortable, like, so obviously we're a small company.
So like there's no ops, CS ops versus this role, right?
So it's like someone's complaining or not filling out the template.
Like, this role would be building systems to, like, make sure we know if people are not doing it.
And then, you know, exactly what we said, like, sit with them.
Why not?
That's the product side of it, which is, like, our team using our workflows.
Like, why are you not using our workflows?
Or why are you not using our linear task project pages?
Yeah.
Then from there, like, okay, now I'm going to make it the whole thing better, right?
Right.
Right.
I don't know.
Is that, like, I just want to make sure that that would be okay?
Because, you know, some people...
like pretty much what I'm looking for.

like, totally I'm aware that a lot of people don't like this whole work in the open remote setup where it's, like, we do in Slack every day.

do their to-do lists.

They're out of my brain.
I come back to it later, set a timer to, like, remind me.
I've got, like, an external.
I use a program called Obsidian where I kind of, like, take notes on things I like to know and would like to know in the future so that I don't have to recall them completely from memory.

I could always just go back and reference and search it.

environment like that, like where it's so growth focused and building foundational systems, I really think it's a great next step.

So if you're not keeping up, you're falling behind.

I know.
I know.

We learned.
Yeah, we learned in our program, sometimes you start out on an angle that's a little easier to sell, and then you use that cash flow to build underneath it.
Yeah, it's kind of the opposite of SaaS.
My old company was the typical SaaS, like burn money to find product market fit.
But then if you do find it, bam, you can sell to like a thousand customers to have great margins.
But most companies burn millions and never find that.
So it's just a very different approach to like company building, for sure.
